summary: Gruber argues that iOS's dominance over Android in developer support is best understood as an ecosystem war, not merely an app war. He identifies three reinforcing advantages: Apple's small number of high-performance devices makes development easier, iOS has deeper software capabilities especially in video and graphics rooted in decades of QuickTime and NeXTStep heritage, and iOS users are demonstrably more engaged and higher-spending customers. He cites Gogo's in-flight wi-fi data showing 84% iOS usage to illustrate that raw market share numbers mislead about actual platform engagement. The conclusion is that pundits focused on Android's global market share are misreading the competitive dynamics entirely.
key_points: The iOS advantage is an ecosystem war (hardware + software + user demographics), not just an app war — framing it as apps alone makes Android look competitive when it isn't
Apple's small number of high-performance devices is a developer sweet spot: many users on few devices is ideal for platform support
iOS has deep software advantages in video playback and GPU acceleration tracing back to QuickTime (1992) and NeXTStep (1989) that Android cannot match
iOS users are measurably better customers who spend more and engage more, making market share comparisons misleading — Gogo data shows 84% iOS usage on flights
Android's raw market share is its strongest developer argument but is insufficient to overcome Apple's combined hardware, software, and demographic advantages
money_quotes: The sweet spot for making a platform appealing to developers is to have many users on a small number of devices. That's iOS.
The elephant in the room: people who choose iOS devices are better customers than people who choose Android.
There's no hypocrisy in believing that all people are created equal, but all customers are not.
iOS can draw a lineage in these regards back to the first versions of QuickTime (1992) and NeXTStep (1989).
Android's raw market share is indeed the best thing Android has going for it, developer-support-wise, but it's nowhere near enough to overcome Apple's remaining advantages.

It's not fair to say that Apple is playing chess while Google, Samsung, Microsoft, and the rest are playing checkers. It's more like they're all playing chess, Apple is winning, but there's a large contingent of the tech and investor commentariat who think the game is checkers and thus are deeply confused.
key_insight: iOS dominates developer support not through any single advantage but through the compounding ecosystem effect of fewer high-end devices, deeper software capabilities, and more engaged customers — a combination that raw market share numbers fundamentally fail to capture.
